peer_reviewed.title: Does Government Ideology Affect Labour Injuries?
peer_reviewed.authors: Mustafa Caglayan, Ahmed Chaudhry, and Firat Demir
peer_reviewed.abstract: Utilizing a comprehensive cross-sectional annual panel dataset spanning 94 countries, we examine the effects of the economic ideology of heads of state on fatal and nonfatal workplace injuries. Based on a two-stage least-squares instrumental variables method, our empirical analysis suggests an increase in fatal and nonfatal injury incidence under right-wing heads of state and a decrease under left-wing heads of state, which are economically relevant.

peer_reviewed.title: Do Limits to Campaign Contribution Laws Affect Welfare Policy? Evidence from the US States
peer_reviewed.citation: The B.E. Journal of Economic Analysis & Policy, 2026.
peer_reviewed.abstract: This paper analyzes how campaign contribution laws influence state-level welfare policies in the US. Previous studies show that policy outcomes often disproportionately reflect the preferences of high-income individuals and organized interest groups over those of low-income groups. The primary channel of this influence over policy is private campaign contributions. However, restricting the amount of money in politics through stricter campaign finance laws reduces donor influence over political candidates and increases legislative representation for low-income groups. Using a panel of US states from 1980 to 2018, the paper examines two key measures of a state's welfare-policy generosity: the real minimum wage and the Earned Income Tax Credit (EITC). The empirical analysis reveals that more stringent campaign contribution laws are associated with significant increases in both the minimum wage and EITC rates. These effects remain robust to a battery of sensitivity tests and alternative measures of state welfare-policy generosity.

peer_reviewed.title: Stay Healthy, Stay Employed: How Flu Vaccination Might Impact Women’s Earnings
peer_reviewed.authors: Pallab Ghosh, Firat Demir, Myongjin Kim, Ahmed Chaudhry, and Junying Zhao
peer_reviewed.abstract: This paper examines the effects of state-level adult and child seasonal flu vaccination rates on hourly real wages for women. Using an instrumental-variables method to address possible endogeneity, we find a significantly positive effect of adult vaccination on women’s wages. This effect is stronger for working mothers in age groups 16–24 and 25–44 and for those with younger children aged 6 months–12 years. We also find that children’s vaccination has a significantly positive effect on mothers’ wages, particularly for non-college-educated women aged 16–44 with at least one child.

peer_reviewed.title: Impacts of the US CDC Recommendation on Human Papillomavirus Vaccine Uptake, 2010–2015
peer_reviewed.authors: Pallab K. Ghosh, Ahmed Chaudhry, Janis E. Campbell, Myongjin Kim, Kyle Smith, Firat Demir, and Junying Zhao
peer_reviewed.abstract: This study evaluates the impact of the US CDC’s HPV vaccine recommendation on male adults and children. Using the 2010–2015 US National Health Interview Survey, fixed-effects regressions show that the policy was associated with a 14.8% increase in the likelihood of HPV vaccination for men aged 11–21. It was also associated with a 5.6% spillover increase for men aged 22–25 and marginal increases for older men. African American men and men in poor health were less likely to receive the vaccine. The findings provide evidence on the effectiveness and spillover effects of federal vaccine recommendations and identify vulnerable subpopulations for future policy interventions.

peer_reviewed.title: Political Competition and Economic Policy: Empirical Evidence from Pakistan
peer_reviewed.citation: Economics: The Open-Access, Open-Assessment E-Journal, 13 (2019-1), pp. 1–25.
peer_reviewed.abstract: The influence of politics on economic policy is not fully understood, and the challenge of ensuring political inclusiveness and economic prosperity remains. This paper gathers empirical evidence from Pakistan, focusing on nine elections held from 1970 to 2015 to measure political competition and estimate its impact on economic policy. Contrary to popular conceptions about Pakistan’s economy, the authors find a positive association between a lack of political competition and poor economic policy. This finding holds at national and subnational levels and withstands a number of robustness tests.

working_papers.title: The Effects of Political Ideology on Export Sophistication and Productive Capabilities
working_papers.abstract: This paper examines how government ideology shapes productive capabilities, focusing on the short- and medium-term effects of closely contested elections. Using a novel dataset on economic partisanship and sectoral productivity across countries, we find that left- and right-wing governments tend to reduce productive capabilities, whereas centrist governments tend to enhance them. These effects are amplified in autocratic regimes and in parliamentary systems. These findings highlight the importance of regime type and government structure in determining how partisan agendas affect the accumulation of productive capabilities and the effectiveness of development-oriented policies.

working_papers.title: The Nexus Between Political Ideology and IMF Interventions: A Reciprocal Relationship
working_papers.abstract: This paper examines how a government’s economic ideology influences its decision to participate in International Monetary Fund programs, how partisanship affects the depth and breadth of IMF conditions, and how IMF programs and conditionality influence which political parties remain in or come to power. Using an instrumental-variable approach, we find that left-wing governments are less likely to enter IMF programs, while right-wing governments are more likely to do so. Left-wing governments face fewer conditionality requirements, whereas right-wing governments face more. A higher burden of conditionality reduces the chances of left-wing or centrist parties staying in or gaining office while increasing those of right-wing parties.

working_papers.title: Who Rules the World? The Myth of Multipolarity and the Real Drivers of Leadership in International Organizations
working_papers.authors: Ahmed Chaudhry, Firat Demir, Omar S. Dahi, Ricardo S. Diaz, Van Jackson, and Kevin Young
working_papers.abstract: The growing prominence of the Global South in trade, finance, and politics has been widely viewed as a structural shift in the global balance of power. This paper challenges that narrative by examining leadership representation in 186 international organizations and more than 5,000 leader-year observations from 1997 to 2022. Traditional indicators of power, such as GDP size and income levels, do not significantly influence leadership shares; global trade and foreign direct investment shares are more important. Representation is overwhelmingly shaped by path dependence, with previous leaders’ nationalities predicting future representation with up to 94% accuracy. Emerging and other Global South nations remain underrepresented even after accounting for economic size and other covariates.

working_papers.title: Beyond the Cost of Living: Inflation and Domestic Violence in Punjab, Pakistan
working_papers.authors: Ahmed Chaudhry, Pallab Ghosh, and Haseeb-Ur-Rehman
